1.description: Risky asset demand. This figure illustrates the optimal portfolio demandX1i(P1)$X_{1i}(P_{1})$, characterized in equation (6), for investorias a function of the asset priceP1for two tax rates, τ0and τ1, such thatτ1>τ0>0$\tau _{1}>\tau _{0}>0$. A linear transaction taxτ>0$\tau >0$is reflected as a higher priceP1(1+τ)$P_{1}(1+\tau )$paid by buyers, a lower priceP1(1−τ)$P_{1}(1-\tau )$received by sellers, and an inaction region that is increasing in τ, all else equal. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

2.description: Individual marginal welfare impact (planner's and investors' perspectives). This figure shows the normalized individual marginal welfare impact of a tax change for specific investors from the perspective of the planner (left plots, see equation (11)) and from the perspective of each individual investor (middle plots, see equation (B3)) for different values of τ. All plots in this figure satisfy Assumption [G], described on page 51, which explicitly definesεdi$\varepsilon _{di}$andεhi$\varepsilon _{hi}$. The right plots show individual asset turnover for specific investors for different values of τ. The top row plots correspond to a buyer (εdi>0$\varepsilon _{di}>0$andεhi=0$\varepsilon _{hi}=0$) and a seller (εdi>0$\varepsilon _{di}>0$andεhi=0$\varepsilon _{hi}=0$) who trade only for nonfundamental reasons. The middle row plots correspond to a buyer (εdi=0$\varepsilon _{di}=0$andεhi<0$\varepsilon _{hi}<0$) and a seller (εdi=0$\varepsilon _{di}=0$andεhi>0$\varepsilon _{hi}>0$) who trade only for fundamental reasons. The bottom plots correspond to a buyer (εdi>0$\varepsilon _{di}>0$andεhi<0$\varepsilon _{hi}<0$) who buys for nonfundamental and fundamental reasons and a seller (εdi<0$\varepsilon _{di}<0$andεhi>0$\varepsilon _{hi}>0$) who sells for nonfundamental and fundamental reasons. The values ofεdi$\varepsilon _{di}$andεhi$\varepsilon _{hi}$used correspond to one standard deviation of the distributions ofεdiP1$\smash{\frac{\varepsilon _{di}}{P_{1}}}$andεhiP1$\smash{\frac{\varepsilon _{hi}}{P_{1}}}$, respectively. All plots use the baseline calibration from SectionIV, that is,δNF=0.3$\delta ^{NF}=0.3$(share of nonfundamental trading volume),ετlogV|τ=0=100$\varepsilon _{\tau }^{\log \mathcal {V}}|_{\tau =0}=100$(laissez-faire semielasticity of volume to taxes), andΠ=1.5%$\Pi =1.5\%$(quarterly risk premium). The plots assume that (i) the planner's mean beliefEp[D]$\mathbb {E}_{p}[D]$equals the average mean beliefμd$\mu _{d}$, and (ii) an individually targeted rebate rule is implemented. See theInternet Appendixfor an illustration of how the results change after relaxing both assumptions. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

3.description: Aggregate marginal welfare impact. This figure shows the normalized aggregate marginal welfare impact of a tax change,dVpdτP1Q$\frac{\frac{dV^{p}}{d\tau }}{P_{1}Q}$, defined in equation (12), for different values of τ. This figure satisfies Assumption [G], described on page 51, and uses the baseline calibration from SectionIV, that is,δNF=0.3$\delta ^{NF}=0.3$(share of nonfundamental trading volume),ετlogV|τ=0=100$\varepsilon _{\tau }^{\log \mathcal {V}}|_{\tau =0}=100$(laissez-faire semi-elasticity of volume to taxes), andΠ=1.5%$\Pi =1.5\%$(quarterly risk premium). The optimal tax, represented by a vertical dotted line, isτ∗=0.37%$\tau ^{*}=0.37\%$. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

4.description: (Failure of quasi-concavity of planner's objective). This figure shows the normalized aggregate welfare impact of a tax change from the planner's perspective in a scenario in which the planner's objective fails to be quasi-concave. This figure corresponds to an environment in which there are two groups of investors with the same degree of risk aversion and the same initial asset holdings; 90% of investors belong to group 1, while the remaining 10% belong to group 2. Group 1 investors have turnover of 1/4 and a share of nonfundamental trading volume of 0.3. Group 2 investors have turnover of 1 and a share of nonfundamental trading volume of 0.65. The risk premium is 1.5%. In this example, the planner's objective has three critical points, with two local maxima atτ=0.67%$\tau =0.67\%$(the globally optimal tax) andτ=3.66%$\tau =3.66\%$, and a local minimum atτ=1.72%$\tau =1.72\%$. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

5.description: Share of nonfundamental trading volume. This figure shows the optimal taxτ∗$\tau ^{*}$as a function of the share of nonfundamental trading volumeδNF$\delta ^{NF}$, defined in equation (20). The sets of reference parameters areδNF=0.3$\delta ^{NF}=0.3$andετlogV|τ=0=−100$\varepsilon _{\tau }^{\log \mathcal {V}}|_{\tau =0}=-100$. The optimal tax for the reference parameters, represented by a vertical dotted line, isτ∗=0.37%$\tau ^{*}=0.37\%$. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

6.description: Volume semielasticity. This figure shows the optimal taxτ∗$\tau ^{*}$as a function of the magnitude of volume semielasticityετlogV=dlogVdτ$\varepsilon _{\tau }^{\log \mathcal {V}}=\frac{d\log \mathcal {V}}{d\tau }$, evaluated atτ=0$\tau =0$. The sets of reference parameters areδNF=0.3$\delta ^{NF}=0.3$andετlogV|τ=0=−100$\varepsilon _{\tau }^{\log \mathcal {V}}|_{\tau =0}=-100$. The optimal tax for the reference parameters, represented by a vertical dotted line, isτ∗=0.37%$\tau ^{*}=0.37\%$. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

7.description: Trading volume implementation. This figure illustrates the volume decomposition established in Proposition2whenετlogV|τ=0=−100$\varepsilon _{\tau }^{\log \mathcal {V}}|_{\tau =0}=-100$andδNF=0.3$\delta^{NF}=0.3$. It shows total turnover, given byΞ(τ)=V(τ)Q$\Xi (\tau )=\frac{{\mathcal {V}}(\tau )}{Q}$, fundamental turnover, given byΞF(τ)=ΘF(τ)P1Q$\Xi _{F}(\tau )=\frac{\Theta _{F}(\tau )}{P_{1}Q}$, nonfundamental turnover, given byΞNF(τ)=ΘNF(τ)P1Q$\Xi _{NF}(\tau )=\frac{\Theta _{NF}(\tau )}{P_{1}Q}$, and the tax-induced turnover reduction,Ξτ(τ)=Θτ(τ)P1Q$\Xi _{\tau }(\tau )=\frac{\Theta _{\tau }(\tau )}{P_{1}Q}$. The optimal tax, represented by a vertical dotted line, isτ∗=0.37%$\tau ^{*}=0.37\%$. Note that this line goes through to the points in whichΞ(τ)$\Xi (\tau )$andΞF(τ)$\Xi _{F}(\tau )$, as well asΞNF(τ)$\Xi _{NF}(\tau )$andΞτ(τ)$\Xi _{\tau }(\tau )$, intersect, consistent with Proposition2b). [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

8.description: Tax revenue. This figure shows total tax revenue per dollar of market capitalization of the risky asset, given by2τV(τ)Q=2τΞ(τ)$2\tau \frac{\mathcal {V}(\tau )}{Q}=2\tau \Xi (\tau )$. The optimal corrective tax isτ∗=0.37%$\tau ^{*}=0.37\%$, while the revenue maximizing tax isτrev=0.78%$\tau ^{\text{rev}}=0.78\%$. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

9.description: Turnover with imperfect enforcement. This figure illustrates the behavior of asset turnover in the extension of the model with imperfect tax enforcement whenψ=1$\psi =1$. The solid dark blue line shows turnover in marketA, the one directly affected by the tax. The dashed light blue line shows turnover in marketB, which starts at zero whenτ=0$\tau =0$. The orange dashed line shows total turnover, given by the sum of turnover in both marketsΞ(τ)=ΞA(τ)+ΞB(τ)$\Xi (\tau )=\Xi ^{A}(\tau )+\Xi ^{B}(\tau )$. The dotted black shows, for reference, total turnover (in marketA) in the baseline model, for whichψ=0$\psi =0$. As expected, turnover in marketAunder imperfect enforcement is lower than under perfect enforcement, but total volume (adding up both markets) is higher. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

10.description: Optimal tax with imperfect enforcement. This figure shows the optimal tax under imperfect enforcementτ∗∗$\tau ^{**}$, defined in equation (22), as a function of the normalized leakage elasticity ψ. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]
